statement.en: The Functional Results of Surgical Percutaneous Joystick Reduction Therapy for Isolated Severely Angulated Radial Neck Fracture in Children
statement.summary: \nBackground: Radial neck fractures in children account for 5 to 10% of all elbow fractures in children. They are extra-articular fractures of the radius proximal to the bicipital tuberosity. The physis is typically involved as a Salter-Harris I or II pattern. Alternatively, the fracture sometimes is extraphyseal, through the metaphysis. In children there is considerable potential for remodeling after these fractures. Up to 30° of radial head tilt and up to 3 mm of transverse displacement are acceptable. Many modalities of treatment are available regarding Surgical &Non-Surgical treatments. Objectives: To evaluate the functional outcome after surgical percutaneous joystick reduction therapy of severely angulated radial neck fracture in children. Type of the study: A prospective study.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a clinical trial protocol/prospective study dated 2026 (future date, likely typographical error for 2024 or under-review manuscript). The axiom proposes a specific surgical intervention (percutaneous joystick reduction) for pediatric radial neck fractures. Classification as FLOWING because: (1) the study appears to be prospective/ongoing rather than completed peer-reviewed research; (2) functional outcomes of surgical interventions require empirical validation across patient cohorts; (3) the categorical assignment (cs.AI) is incongruent with medical/orthopedic content, suggesting metadata error or preprint status.
